meta.title: 7.2 Real Scenarios
meta.description: Apply your code literacy to 8 realistic, role-specific situations — from adding tracking code to estimating feature complexity.

blocks.content:
## Putting code literacy to work This module presents 8 realistic scenarios. Each one puts you in a specific role where code literacy makes the difference. Read each scenario carefully, look at the code, and think about what you would do. --- ### Scenario 1: The Marketer Adding Tracking Code You are a marketing manager. Your analytics vendor sent you a tracking snippet to add to your company's landing page. They said: "Paste this just before the closing `` tag." Here is the top of your page's source: ```html Summer Sale - ShopBright

Summer Sale - 40% Off Everything

``` And here is the snippet they sent: ```html ``` You know that `` is the behind-the-scenes section and that `

problems.question:
In Scenario 1, the marketing manager needs to add a tracking snippet. Where should it go?

```html
<head>
  <title>Summer Sale</title>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">
  <!-- where does the snippet go? -->
</head>
```
options: A) Just before the closing </head> tag, after the existing <link> element
B) Inside the <body> tag, right after the <header>
C) Before the opening <html> tag at the very top of the file
problems.explanation: The vendor said to paste it before the closing </head> tag, and this makes sense — tracking scripts belong in the <head> so they load early. Placing it after the existing elements in the <head> section is correct.

problems.question: In Scenario 2, the PM found that the Add to Cart button is invisible on mobile. What was the actual cause?
options: A) The click event listener does not work on touch devices
B) A CSS media query sets the button's parent container to display: none below 768px
C) The mouseover event is blocking the click event on mobile browsers
problems.explanation: The JavaScript click handler works fine on mobile. The problem is a CSS media query that hides the entire .product-actions container on viewports below 768px. The button is functional — it is just invisible because its parent is hidden.

problems.question: In Scenario 3, the designer found an issue with the AI-generated card component. What was the visual problem?
options: A) The padding was wrong — it should be 12px instead of 16px
B) The image's border-radius only rounds the top corners, so it visually breaks the card's rounded shape at the bottom
C) The box-shadow was too strong and did not match the mockup's subtle style
problems.explanation: The image has border-radius only on the top corners (12px 12px 0 0), which means its bottom corners are square. When the image sits inside a card with fully rounded corners, the bottom of the image can visually clash. The fix is to add overflow: hidden on the card or match the border-radius on all corners.

problems.question: In Scenario 4, the founder reviewed the contractor's code. Which of these is NOT a problem with the delivery?
options: A) The page uses only <div> elements with no semantic HTML
B) The class names are single letters like 'a', 'b', 'c' instead of meaningful names
C) The page is missing a <head> section with a <title> tag
problems.explanation: The code snippet shows the <body> content. While a missing <head> could be a problem, the scenario specifically highlights three issues: no semantic HTML (all divs), meaningless class names, and no accessibility. The <head> section was not shown and is not one of the flagged issues in this scenario.

problems.question: In Scenario 5, why was the YouTube embed broken?
options: A) The <iframe> tag was misspelled and the browser could not render it
B) The src URL used the regular watch format instead of the /embed/ format that YouTube requires for iframes
C) The width and height attributes were too large for the container
problems.explanation: YouTube embed URLs must use the format youtube.com/embed/VIDEO_ID, not the regular youtube.com/watch?v=VIDEO_ID format. The watch URL is for viewing in a browser tab, not for embedding in an iframe.

problems.question: In Scenario 6, the product owner found two issues with the signup form's email field. What were they?
options: A) The input uses type="text" instead of type="email" and is missing the required attribute
B) The input is missing a placeholder attribute and a name attribute
C) The label's for attribute does not match the input's id attribute
problems.explanation: The email input uses type="text" (which accepts any text) instead of type="email" (which triggers browser format validation). It is also missing the required attribute, so the form can be submitted with an empty email field. The password field correctly has required — the email field should match.

problems.question: In Scenario 7, why can't the team lead just say 'yes, changing the button color is quick'?
options: A) CSS cannot change button colors — it requires JavaScript
B) The button color uses a CSS variable from a design system, so changing it affects every primary button site-wide
C) The button is an image, not styled with CSS, so a designer needs to create a new image
problems.explanation: The button uses var(--brand-primary), a CSS custom property defined elsewhere (likely in a design system or theme file). Changing that variable is technically simple, but it changes the color of every element that references it — not just one button. The team lead correctly flags that this needs broader review.

problems.question: In Scenario 8, after deploying the static site, images are not loading. What should you check first?
options: A) Open DevTools and check the Console for 404 errors to see if the image file paths are wrong
B) Re-export the images from your design tool in a different format
C) Add a CDN service to host the images separately from the HTML
problems.explanation: When resources do not load after deploying, the most common cause is incorrect file paths. The DevTools Console shows 404 (not found) errors that tell you exactly which files the browser cannot find. Check that the paths in your tags match the actual file names and folder structure on the server.

questions: Which of the 8 scenarios felt most relevant to your actual role or daily work? Why?
In which scenario would you have been completely stuck before taking this course? What specific knowledge from the course would help you now?
Think of a real situation in your work that is similar to one of these scenarios. How would you handle it now?

blocks.content: Code literacy shows up in everyday work situations — not just when you are building something. Whether you are adding tracking code, filing bug reports, reviewing deliverables, or estimating effort, the ability to read and understand code gives you an informed perspective that leads to better decisions and clearer communication.

blocks.prompt: You have seen how code literacy applies across roles and situations. Now it is time for the final challenge: a complete, buggy webpage that tests everything you have learned. Module 7.3 is your capstone — let's see what you can do.

reviewQuestions.question: Code literacy helps in non-developer roles by enabling you to:
options: Write all the code yourself
File precise bug reports, review deliverables, estimate effort, and communicate with developers effectively
Replace the engineering team entirely
reviewQuestions.explanation: Code literacy lets you communicate more precisely with developers, understand what's possible, estimate effort realistically, and catch issues early.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly! It's about informed communication, not doing the coding yourself.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Code literacy enables better bug reports, deliverable reviews, effort estimates, and developer communication — without writing code yourself.

reviewQuestions.question: When filing a bug report, saying 'the button on the checkout page returns a 404 in the Network tab' is better than 'the page is broken' because:
options: It uses more words
It tells the developer exactly what element, what page, and what evidence you found
It sounds more professional
reviewQuestions.explanation: Specific details (which element, which page, what DevTools showed) let developers reproduce and fix the issue immediately instead of guessing.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right! Specificity saves everyone time and leads to faster fixes.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Specific bug reports name the element, the page, and the evidence. This lets developers go straight to the problem instead of hunting for it.
